title: The advice we needed but ignored
summary: Describe a piece of advice you received at some point in your life that you chose not to follow, and reflect on what happened as a result.
topic: The advice we needed but ignored
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) about a time you received advice that you did not follow, and what you learned from ignoring it. Use at least one relative clause, a conditional sentence, the present perfect for reflection, and 'however' or 'although' for concession.
hintStarters: There was a moment when someone I trusted tried to warn me about something, and instead of listening I…
Looking back now, I can see exactly why the advice made sense, but at the time I was convinced that…
If I had paid more attention to what she told me that afternoon, I would probably have avoided…

hintVocab.meaningVi: có thiện ý, xuất phát từ lòng tốt
nhìn lại, sau khi sự việc đã xảy ra
ghi nhớ và tiếp thu điều gì đó
chấp nhận và đối mặt với điều gì đó
cảnh báo ai đó không nên làm gì

sampleText:
When I was seventeen, my uncle sat me down and told me not to quit the football team. I had decided that training three evenings a week was too much alongside school, and I wanted to use that time for other things. He was a man who had given up his own sport at a similar age and always regretted it, so his advice came from a place I should have respected. However, I told him I had already made up my mind, and I left the team the following week.

Although I felt relieved at first — my evenings were suddenly free and schoolwork seemed easier to manage — the relief did not last long. By the middle of the school year I had stopped exercising altogether, and I felt the difference quickly. I had also lost the group of friends that came with the team, which I had not anticipated. The extra time I had gained was not being used well; I was simply spending more hours on my phone. My uncle had warned me against exactly this kind of drift, but I had brushed aside his concern as old-fashioned. In hindsight, he understood something about routine and structure that I was not yet ready to admit: that free time without a purpose tends to disappear into nothing useful.

I have thought about that conversation many times since then. What I have come to understand is that the advice we ignore is usually the advice that challenges something we have already decided. I did not want to hear what my uncle was saying because I had already made a choice and wanted it confirmed, not questioned. If I had been more honest with myself about why I was quitting — not time, really, but the pressure of a competitive coach — I might have looked for a different solution. The lesson I took from that year was not simply that I should have stayed on the team. It was that well-meaning advice deserves at least a moment of genuine consideration before it is dismissed. That is something I have tried to carry with me ever since, even when I find it uncomfortable to do so.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: came from a place I should have respected
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: xuất phát từ một lý do tôi đáng lẽ phải tôn trọng

sampleAnnotations.phrase: I had already made up my mind

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: ban đầu cảm thấy nhẹ nhõm

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the relief did not last long
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: cảm giác nhẹ nhõm đó không kéo dài

sampleAnnotations.phrase: warned me against exactly this kind of drift
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: đã cảnh báo tôi chính xác về kiểu buông lơi này
gạt bỏ mối lo lắng của ông

sampleAnnotations.phrase: free time without a purpose tends to disappear into nothing useful
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: thời gian rảnh không có mục đích thường trôi đi mà chẳng ích lợi gì

sampleAnnotations.phrase: what I have come to understand
challenges something we have already decided
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: thách thức điều chúng ta đã quyết định
muốn được xác nhận, không phải bị đặt câu hỏi
tìm kiếm một giải pháp khác

sampleAnnotations.phrase: deserves at least a moment of genuine consideration
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: xứng đáng được suy nghĩ thật sự ít nhất một lần
mang theo bên mình từ đó đến nay

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Third conditional for past regret
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If I had been more honest with myself about why I was quitting, I might have looked for a different solution' dùng 'had + past participle' trong mệnh đề 'if' và 'might have + past participle' trong mệnh đề chính — đây là điều kiện loại 3, diễn đạt điều có thể đã xảy ra khác đi trong quá khứ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If I had been more honest with myself about why I was quitting, I might have looked for a different solution' uses 'had + past participle' in the if-clause and 'might have + past participle' in the main clause — a third conditional expressing what could have happened differently in the past.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for ongoing realisation
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I have thought about that conversation many times since then' và 'what I have come to understand' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để diễn đạt hành động hoặc sự nhận thức bắt đầu trong quá khứ và vẫn còn liên quan đến hiện tại — được nhấn mạnh bởi 'since then'.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I have thought about that conversation many times since then' and 'what I have come to understand' use the present perfect to express an action or realisation that began in the past and is still relevant now — reinforced by the time marker 'since then'.

sampleTranslationVi:
Khi tôi mười bảy tuổi, chú tôi ngồi lại và bảo tôi đừng bỏ đội bóng đá. Tôi đã quyết định rằng tập luyện ba buổi tối mỗi tuần là quá nhiều khi còn đang học, và tôi muốn dùng khoảng thời gian đó cho những việc khác. Ông là người đã từ bỏ môn thể thao của mình ở độ tuổi tương tự và luôn tiếc nuối điều đó, vì vậy lời khuyên của ông xuất phát từ một lý do tôi đáng lẽ phải tôn trọng. Tuy nhiên, tôi nói với ông rằng tôi đã quyết định rồi, và tôi rời đội vào tuần sau.

Mặc dù ban đầu tôi cảm thấy nhẹ nhõm — các buổi tối đột nhiên rảnh rang và việc học dường như dễ quản lý hơn — cảm giác nhẹ nhõm đó không kéo dài. Đến giữa năm học, tôi đã hoàn toàn ngừng tập thể dục, và tôi cảm nhận được sự khác biệt rất nhanh. Tôi cũng mất đi nhóm bạn bè gắn liền với đội, điều mà tôi không lường trước được. Thời gian rảnh mà tôi có thêm không được sử dụng tốt; tôi chỉ đơn giản là dành thêm nhiều giờ trên điện thoại. Chú tôi đã cảnh báo tôi về chính kiểu buông lơi này, nhưng tôi đã gạt bỏ mối lo ngại của ông như là cổ hủ. Nhìn lại, ông đã hiểu điều gì đó về thói quen và cấu trúc mà tôi chưa sẵn sàng thừa nhận: rằng thời gian rảnh không có mục đích thường trôi đi mà chẳng ích lợi gì.

Tôi đã nghĩ về cuộc trò chuyện đó nhiều lần kể từ đó. Điều tôi dần nhận ra là lời khuyên mà chúng ta bỏ qua thường là lời khuyên thách thức điều chúng ta đã quyết định. Tôi không muốn nghe điều chú tôi nói vì tôi đã đưa ra quyết định và muốn được xác nhận, không phải bị đặt câu hỏi. Nếu tôi thành thật hơn với bản thân về lý do thực sự bỏ đội — không phải thời gian, mà thực ra là áp lực từ huấn luyện viên cạnh tranh — có lẽ tôi đã tìm kiếm một giải pháp khác. Bài học tôi rút ra từ năm đó không chỉ đơn giản là tôi đáng lẽ nên ở lại trong đội. Mà là lời khuyên chân thành xứng đáng được suy nghĩ thật sự ít nhất một lần trước khi bị bác bỏ. Đó là điều tôi đã cố gắng mang theo bên mình từ đó đến nay, dù đôi khi tôi thấy khó chịu khi làm vậy.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a relative clause correctly?
options: He was a man who had given up his own sport at a similar age.
He was a man which had given up his own sport at a similar age.
He was a man who he had given up his own sport at a similar age.
He was a man that he had given up his own sport at a similar age.
mcQuestions.explanation: Use 'who' for people in relative clauses; do not add a repeated subject pronoun like 'he' inside the clause.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Who' introduces a relative clause for people; do not repeat the subject with 'he'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly forms a third conditional?
options: If I had been more honest with myself, I might have looked for a different solution.
If I was more honest with myself, I might have looked for a different solution.
If I had been more honest with myself, I might look for a different solution.
If I had been more honest with myself, I would look for a different solution.
mcQuestions.explanation: Third conditional: 'had + past participle' in the if-clause and 'would/might + have + past participle' in the main clause.
mcQuestions.hint: Past perfect in the if-clause; 'might have + past participle' in the main clause.

options: Although I felt relieved at first, the relief did not last long.
Although I felt relieved at first but the relief did not last long.
Although, I felt relieved at first, the relief did not last long.
I felt relieved at first although but the relief did not last long.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no extra 'but', and no comma directly after 'although'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Although' introduces a concessive clause; do not add 'but' in the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly?
options: I have thought about that conversation many times since then.
I have think about that conversation many times since then.
I thought about that conversation many times since then.
I have been thinking about that conversation many times since then.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect = 'have/has + past participle'; 'thought' is the past participle of 'think'. 'Since then' confirms the present perfect is needed.
mcQuestions.hint: With 'since then', use the present perfect: 'have + past participle'.

options: My uncle had warned me against exactly this kind of drift.
My uncle had warned me about against exactly this kind of drift.
My uncle had warned me from exactly this kind of drift.
My uncle had warned me of against exactly this kind of drift.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Warn someone against' is the correct collocation when advising someone not to do or risk something.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Warn against' means to advise not to do something; no extra preposition is needed.

options: I brushed aside his concern as old-fashioned.
I brushed away his concern as old-fashioned.
I brushed off aside his concern as old-fashioned.
I brushed his concern as old-fashioned aside.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Brush aside' means to dismiss or ignore; the object goes between 'brush' and 'aside' or after 'aside' for noun phrases.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Brush aside' = to dismiss; 'brush away' and 'brush off' have different meanings.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'come to + infinitive' for gradual understanding?
options: What I have come to understand is that ignored advice usually challenges a decision already made.
What I have come understanding is that ignored advice usually challenges a decision already made.
What I have come to understood is that ignored advice usually challenges a decision already made.
What I came to understand is that ignored advice usually challenges a decision already made.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have come to + base verb' expresses a gradual realisation; use present perfect for a process still relevant now.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Come to understand' = gradually realise; use present perfect 'have come to' for ongoing relevance.

options: Free time without a purpose tends to disappear into nothing useful.
Free time without a purpose tends disappearing into nothing useful.
Free time without a purpose tends to disappearing into nothing useful.
Free time without a purpose is tending to disappear into nothing useful.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tend to + base verb' is the correct structure; no gerund or continuous form is needed after 'tend to'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Tend to' is followed by the base form of the verb, not the gerund.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'deserve + gerund' versus 'deserve + infinitive'?
options: Well-meaning advice deserves at least a moment of genuine consideration before it is dismissed.
Well-meaning advice deserves to having at least a moment of genuine consideration.
Well-meaning advice is deserving at least a moment of genuine consideration.
Well-meaning advice deserves at least a moment to consider genuine.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Deserve + noun phrase' is standard; 'a moment of genuine consideration' is the noun phrase object of 'deserves'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Deserve' can take a noun phrase as its object; no gerund or infinitive is needed here.

options: In hindsight, he understood something about routine that I was not yet ready to admit.
In the hindsight, he understood something about routine that I was not yet ready to admit.
By hindsight, he understood something about routine that I was not yet ready to admit.
With hindsight he understood something about routine that I was not yet ready to admit.
mcQuestions.explanation: The fixed phrase is 'in hindsight' (no article); it is used as a sentence adverb, usually followed by a comma.
mcQuestions.hint: 'In hindsight' has no article; it means 'looking back now with a clearer understanding'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'make up one's mind'?
options: I told him I had already made up my mind.
I told him I had already made up mind.
I told him I had already made my mind up.
I told him I had already made up the mind.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Make up one's mind' is a fixed phrase meaning to decide; the possessive (my/your/his) cannot be dropped or replaced with 'the'.
mcQuestions.hint: The fixed phrase is 'make up + possessive + mind'; both 'make up my mind' and 'make my mind up' are acceptable, but option A is clearest.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is it possible that some advice we ignored was actually wrong for us, even if the person giving it meant well — and how can we tell the difference only in hindsight?
